entries.title: Raffles — Introduction and Types of Permitted Raffles
entries.original_text:
A raffle is a lottery scheme where tickets are sold for a chance to win a prize in a draw. The different types of raffle schemes are usually identified by the method of determining the winner. Raffle prizes may consist of merchandise or cash, or a combination of the two.

This chapter outlines the policies and procedures for licensing the following permitted raffle lottery events:
• Stub Draw
• Elimination Draw
• Calendar Draw
• "Golf Ball Drop" Raffle Lottery
• "Rubber Duck" Race
• 50/50 Draw (paper-based)
• Blanket Raffle Licence
• Meat Spin/Turkey Roll
• "Name the Raffle" Lottery
• Bossy Bingo/Cow Patty Bingo
• Auction-Style Raffles
• Catch the Ace Progressive Raffle Lottery
• Electronic raffles.

entries.title: Raffles — Provincial Licensing Authority
entries.original_text:
The Registrar has sole authority to issue lottery licences for raffles where the total value of the prizes to be awarded is greater than $50,000 or where the raffle lottery is to be conducted and managed in conjunction with another licensed gaming event.

Only the Registrar may issue a raffle licence permitting an eligible organization to conduct a raffle lottery event in an unorganized territory, on Crown lands or in First Nations communities that do not have their own Order in Council.

Only the Registrar issues licences for electronic raffles, regardless of prize amount.

entries.title: Raffles — Municipal Licensing Authority
entries.original_text:
Municipalities may only issue licences permitting eligible organizations to conduct non-electronic raffle lottery events where the total value of all the prizes to be awarded is $50,000 or less.

A raffle lottery may only be licensed with the consent of the municipal council by the passage of a resolution or by-law authorizing the issuance of lottery licences, or where the municipality has entered into an agreement with a neighbouring municipality that authorizes the issuance of lottery licences. Municipalities that pass a resolution or by-law may designate a lottery licensing officer.

entries.title: Raffles — General Raffle Licensing Policies (1-9)
entries.original_text:
1. Only eligible organizations may apply for a raffle lottery licence (see "3.1.1. Eligibility to apply for a lottery licence" for further information).

2. Each raffle lottery event requires a separate licence application.

3. An applicant must agree to all terms and conditions of the licence and not to conduct the lottery in a manner that is not in accordance with the application for the licence.

4. An applicant must not submit false or misleading information on their application.

5. The licensing authority has the discretion to impose additional terms and conditions on a licence. Licensees must comply with these terms and conditions.

6. Licensees must disclose any changes to the application, including changes to information such as officers, executive members, and designated bona fide members, and including any changes in criminal charges and/or convictions.

7. An applicant must not conduct advertising, promotion or selling of raffle lottery tickets until the licensing authority has issued the raffle lottery licence.

8. The use of consignment ticket sales is not permitted.

9. Raffle lottery licensees must not use 1-900 numbers (pay-per-call), or equivalent automated telephone services, as a means of selling tickets.

entries.title: Raffles — General Raffle Licensing Policies (10-17)
entries.original_text:
10. Only individuals 18 or older may sell raffle tickets.

11. Raffle lottery tickets may only be purchased using legal tender, cheque, debit card, credit card or money order. The use of electronic or crypto-currency is not permitted.

12. Raffle ticket purchases must not be charged to a third party's credit card unless the cardholder is present and authorizes the transaction.

13. Any monies generated through any fundraising activities, whether or not the activities are licensed, must not be directed to activities associated with terrorism or terrorist financing.

14. The licensee may only sell raffle lottery tickets from permitted locations (see "3.3.1. Ticket Sales" for further information).

15. The licensee must adhere to the permitted total expenses for each raffle lottery event. (See "3.6.5 Expenses—maximum permitted expense rates" for total expense limits.)

16. The licensee must use the proceeds of a licensed raffle lottery event for the purpose(s) approved by the licensing authority. (See "3.6.6. Use of proceeds" for further information.)

17. AGCO licensees must comply with applicable Registrar's Standards for the Conduct and Management of Lottery Events.

entries.title: Raffles — Conflict of Interest Guidelines
entries.original_text:
Board members, executive members, their immediate family members, and the bona fide members designated to be in charge of the conduct and management of the lottery event are prohibited from purchasing tickets for the lottery event that they are conducting and managing.

The licensing authority is obligated to enforce these guidelines with respect to all lottery events.

Licensees are obligated to ensure that all necessary measures are taken so that the drawing of prizes shall be conducted solely on the basis of chance. Should it be found that a lottery has not been conducted on the basis of chance, the licensee could be subject to sanctions (see "3.4.2. Enforcement and Compliance"), and the participant(s) may be subject to penalties under the Criminal Code.

The gaming industry is committed to maintaining the integrity of all lottery events conducted in the province. To this end, the AGCO has developed Conflict of Interest guidelines. These guidelines are available on the AGCO's website.

entries.title: Stub Draw — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
All tickets must be sold prior to the draw. At the time of the draw, all stubs must be placed in a receptacle and must be mechanically mixed. The drawn stub is the winning ticket.

The draw must be conducted at a public event, and at the time and place specified on the ticket and in the licence. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draw.

In the event that the holder of the drawn ticket is not present at the draw, the prize may be claimed by the ticket holder within a specified time frame after the draw. The time frame and prize claim procedures must be established in the licence and printed on the ticket.

The licensee must post a list of winners at its place of business, in a location publicly visible, no later than 7 days after the draw.

entries.title: Elimination Draw — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
An elimination draw is a draw in which tickets are randomly drawn, one at a time, and the holder of the last ticket drawn is the winner.

A ticket number is drawn at random. If the ticket holder is present, the ticket is "eliminated" and the holder is still eligible for the draw for the major prize. If the ticket holder is not present, the ticket is put back in the receptacle and another ticket is drawn.

This process of elimination continues until the prescribed number of ticket holders has been eliminated. A final draw is then conducted to determine the winner of the major prize. The holder of the final ticket drawn wins the major prize.

At the time of the draw, all stubs must be placed in a receptacle and must be mechanically mixed. The draw must be conducted at a public event and at the time and place specified on the ticket and in the licence. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draw.

entries.title: Calendar Draw — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
A calendar draw allows the holder of the winning ticket an opportunity to enter a series of draws over a specific calendar period. Tickets are sold prior to the first draw. The first draw determines the holder who may then enter subsequent draws. The holder of the winning ticket may enter subsequent draws, held over a specific calendar period, for larger prizes. The holder of the last winning ticket in the series wins the major prize.

A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise each draw. In the event that all tickets have not been sold by the date of the first draw, the licensee must conduct the first draw with the tickets sold to that point.

entries.title: Golf Ball Drop Raffle — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
In a Golf Ball Drop raffle lottery, numbered balls (typically golf balls) are released simultaneously from a container. The ball that goes into or closest to a receptacle (such as a hole on a golf course) determines the winning ticket.

All balls must be numbered prior to the event. Each ball corresponds to a ticket number. All balls must be dropped simultaneously, in public, and at the time, date and location specified on the ticket and in the licence. If a ball does not go into or near the receptacle, a draw must be held to determine the winner. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draw.

Licensees conducting a "Golf Ball Drop" raffle lottery event may use a helicopter to drop the balls. The use of a helicopter must comply with all applicable Transport Canada regulations.

entries.title: Rubber Duck Race — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
In a "Rubber Duck" Race, numbered objects (typically rubber ducks) are released simultaneously from a starting point and are carried by a current of water (the body of water to be used must be specified in the application). The object that crosses a finish line (or reaches a specified point) first determines the winning ticket.

All objects must be numbered prior to the event. Each object corresponds to a ticket number. All objects must be released simultaneously, in public, and at the time, date and location specified on the ticket and in the licence. If no object crosses the finish line, a draw must be held to determine the winner. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the event.

The licensee must ensure the event is conducted safely and that all objects are recovered after the event.

entries.title: 50/50 Draw (Paper-Based) — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
In a 50/50 draw, the winning ticket holder receives 50% of the gross ticket revenue as the prize. The remaining 50% is retained by the licensee as gross proceeds.

The draw must be conducted at a public event and at the time and place specified on the ticket and in the licence. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draw. At the time of the draw, all stubs must be placed in a receptacle and must be mechanically mixed.

The prize must be paid from ticket revenue and must be calculated as 50% of the gross ticket revenue. The licensee must not guarantee a minimum prize amount unless the guaranteed amount is covered by a financial guarantee acceptable to the licensing authority.

A 50/50 draw may be conducted at a sporting event or other public event, provided the draw is conducted at the event. The licensee must ensure that all ticket sales cease before the draw is conducted.

Example: Maximum prize board calculation for a 50/50 draw:
If the licensee prints 1,000 tickets at $2 each, the maximum gross ticket revenue would be $2,000 and the maximum prize would be $1,000 (50% of $2,000). The licence fee would be calculated on the maximum prize board of $1,000.

entries.title: Blanket Raffle Licence — Rules and Procedures
Meat Spin/Turkey Roll — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
A meat spin (also known as a "turkey roll") is a raffle lottery event in which meat or food items are offered as prizes. The draw is typically conducted using a wheel, and prize winners are determined by a randomly drawn ticket.

The draw must be conducted at a public event. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draw. All tickets must be sold prior to the draw. At the time of the draw, all stubs must be placed in a receptacle and must be mechanically mixed.

The total retail value of the meat prizes must be stated in the licence application.

entries.title: Name the Raffle Lottery — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
A "Name the Raffle" lottery is a raffle event where the organization gives its event a unique name. The structure and rules are the same as a standard stub draw raffle, but the event is marketed under a custom name chosen by the organization.

All standard raffle policies apply to "Name the Raffle" lottery events. The draw must be conducted at a public event and at the time and place specified on the ticket and in the licence. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draw.

entries.title: Bossy Bingo / Cow Patty Bingo — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
In a "Bossy Bingo" (also known as "Cow Patty Bingo") raffle, a field or area is divided into numbered sections (typically a grid). Each section corresponds to a ticket number. A cow (or similar animal) is released into the field, and the section in which the animal first deposits a "patty" determines the winning ticket.

The field must be clearly divided and numbered prior to the event. All sections must be of equal size. The event must be conducted in public and at the time, date and location specified on the ticket and in the licence. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the event.

If the animal does not produce a result within a reasonable time, a standard stub draw must be held to determine the winner.

entries.title: Auction-Style Raffles — Rules and Procedures
entries.original_text:
An auction-style raffle is a raffle event where multiple prizes are offered and ticket purchasers place their ticket stubs in the receptacle for the prize(s) they wish to win. A separate draw is conducted for each prize.

Ticket purchasers may distribute their ticket stubs among as many prize receptacles as they wish. Each ticket stub may only be placed in one receptacle. The draw for each prize must be conducted at a public event and at the time and place specified on the ticket and in the licence.

A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise the draws. All stubs in each receptacle must be mechanically mixed before the draw for that prize.

entries.title: Catch the Ace Progressive Raffle — Overview and General Rules
entries.original_text:
A Catch the Ace raffle is a progressive raffle lottery in which participants purchase tickets for weekly draws. Each week, a ticket is drawn and the holder selects a card from a board of playing cards. If the selected card is the Ace of Spades, the holder wins the progressive jackpot. If the selected card is not the Ace of Spades, the holder wins a consolation prize and the jackpot carries over to the next week's draw, growing progressively larger.

The following policies apply to Catch the Ace progressive raffle lotteries:

(i) General policies:
A Catch the Ace progressive raffle lottery may be licensed for a maximum of 52 weekly draws (one year). The licensee must sell tickets for each weekly draw. Tickets from a previous week's draw are not valid for subsequent draws — new tickets must be purchased each week.

The licensee must establish a progressive jackpot, which starts at a base amount set by the licensee and grows each week by a percentage of that week's gross ticket revenue (typically 50%). The jackpot is won when a participant selects the Ace of Spades.

If the Ace of Spades is not selected by the end of the licence period (52 weeks or fewer, as specified in the licence), the licensee must conduct a final draw for the full progressive jackpot.

(ii) Card board requirements:
The playing card board must consist of 52 standard playing cards, plus Jokers if applicable. The cards must be sealed in individual envelopes, affixed to a board, and displayed in a manner visible to the public. Once a card is selected and revealed, it is removed from the board and displayed separately. The selected card must not be replaced.

The card board must be kept in a secure location between draws, accessible only to designated bona fide members.

entries.title: Catch the Ace Progressive Raffle — Draw Procedures and Jackpot Rules
entries.original_text:
(iii) Draw procedures:
Each weekly draw must be conducted at a public event and at the time and place specified in the licence. A minimum of two designated bona fide members must supervise each draw. At the time of the draw, all stubs must be placed in a receptacle and must be mechanically mixed.

The drawn ticket holder must be present to select a card from the board. If the drawn ticket holder is not present, the licensee may draw additional tickets until a ticket holder who is present is found. The licensee must establish rules for how many additional draws will be conducted if no present ticket holder is found.

(iv) Consolation prizes:
Each week, the holder of the drawn ticket wins a consolation prize, regardless of whether the Ace of Spades is selected. The consolation prize is typically a percentage of that week's gross ticket revenue (e.g., 10-20%). The consolation prize amount or calculation method must be specified in the licence.

(v) Amendments:
Despite the general rule that amendments must be requested before ticket sales have begun, for Catch the Ace progressive raffles, the licensing authority may consider amendments after ticket sales have begun, on a case-by-case basis, where the amendment is in the public interest.

(vi) Financial reporting:
The licensee must submit interim financial reports as required by the licensing authority, in addition to a final financial report at the conclusion of the licence period.

entries.title: Electronic Raffles — Licensing and General Policies
entries.original_text:
Electronic raffles are raffle lottery events where tickets are sold electronically (e.g., online, through an app, or through electronic terminals). Only the Registrar may issue licences for electronic raffles, regardless of prize amount.

The following policies apply to electronic raffles:

(i) Paid sellers:
A licensee conducting an electronic raffle may use paid sellers to sell electronic raffle tickets, subject to the following conditions:
• Paid sellers must be registered under the Gaming Control Act, 1992, or be employees of a registered gaming-related supplier.
• The licensee must maintain a list of all paid sellers and provide it to the Registrar upon request.
• Paid sellers must not be members of the licensee organization's board of directors or executive.

(ii) Problem gambling:
The licensee must implement responsible gambling measures, including:
• Providing information about problem gambling resources on the electronic raffle platform.
• Allowing purchasers to set voluntary spending limits.
• Providing a self-exclusion mechanism.

(iii) Sponsorships:
A licensee may enter into sponsorship agreements related to an electronic raffle, provided that the sponsorship does not influence the outcome of the raffle or the selection of winners. All sponsorship agreements must be disclosed to the Registrar.

entries.title: Electronic Raffles — Subscriptions and Hospital Foundation E-Raffles
entries.original_text:
(iv) Subscriptions:
A licensee conducting an electronic raffle may offer subscription-based ticket purchases, where a purchaser agrees to purchase tickets for multiple draws in advance. The following conditions apply:
• The purchaser must be able to cancel the subscription at any time.
• The licensee must provide a confirmation of each ticket purchase to the subscriber.
• The subscription terms must be clearly disclosed to the purchaser before the first purchase.

(v) Hospital Foundation e-Raffles:
Hospital foundations that are eligible organizations may apply for electronic raffle licences. In addition to the general electronic raffle policies, the following apply:
• The hospital foundation must demonstrate that the proceeds will be used for hospital-related charitable purposes.
• The electronic raffle platform must comply with all applicable accessibility standards.
• The hospital foundation may partner with a registered gaming-related supplier to operate the electronic raffle platform.

entries.title: Prohibited Raffle Events
entries.original_text:
The following raffle events cannot be licensed:

(A) Sports pools:
Sports pools are not permitted to be licensed as raffle lotteries. A sports pool is a lottery scheme where participants select or are assigned teams or players, and prizes are awarded based on the outcomes of sporting events. Sports pools are considered to be sports betting and cannot be licensed under the Criminal Code provisions that authorize lottery licensing.

(B) Raffles by donation:
Raffle events where tickets are distributed in exchange for a donation (i.e., where there is no fixed ticket price) cannot be licensed. Raffle tickets must have a fixed price, and all ticket purchasers must pay the same price.

(C) Scratch and win:
"Scratch and win" or instant-win lottery events cannot be licensed as raffle lotteries. These events are considered to be a different form of lottery scheme and are reserved for the provincial government (Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation).

entries.title: Municipal Licensing Policies for Raffles
entries.original_text:
In addition to the general raffle licensing policies set out in Section 5.1.2, the following policies apply to licences issued by municipalities:

1. If a licensee wishes to sell tickets from an established point of sale or door-to-door in more than one municipality, it must obtain permission from each municipality in which it proposes to sell tickets.

2. A charitable organization applying to conduct and manage a raffle lottery event must submit its application to the municipality in which it wishes to conduct and manage the event. The application must include a letter of support from the home base municipality. The municipality may or may not issue the licence pursuant to Section 2.(a) of the Order-in-Council 208/2024.

3. A municipality may issue a raffle licence permitting an eligible organization to use a special occasion gaming event (which must be licensed by the Registrar) as a venue for a raffle draw, if the raffle licence clearly states that ticket sales must end prior to the conduct and management of the special occasion gaming event.

entries.title: Municipal Licensing Procedures for Raffles
Municipal Licensing Fees for Raffles
entries.original_text:
The municipality may set up a fee structure to cover the cost of issuing licences and any additional costs related to the supervision and control of the licensed lottery events.

The fee for a municipal raffle lottery licence must not exceed the maximum amount set by the Registrar.

In the case of a 50/50 draw, the licence fee is calculated based on the maximum prize board (see "5.2.1(F) 50/50 draw (paper-based)" for an example).

Please see "Chapter 10: Bingo—in Pooling Bingo Halls", for fees applicable to raffles conducted and managed in pooling bingo halls operating under the Bingo Revenue Model.

entries.title: Provincial Licensing Policies for Raffles
entries.original_text:
In addition to the general raffle lottery licensing policies set out in Section 5.1.1, the following policies apply to raffle licences issued by the Registrar:

1. The applicant organization must notify the municipality in which the organization is based when obtaining a raffle licence issued by the Registrar.

2. In the case of an incomplete application, the licensing official will notify the applicant of the deficiencies in the application and request all the required documents.

3. If an applicant intends to award prizes of one million dollars or more, the application must also include:
» a summary of the applicant's experience in conducting and managing raffle lotteries.
» a business plan
» a budget
» a ticket sales plan, including an explanation of how credit card and debit card sales as well as dishonoured cheques will be handled.

4. If a licensee wishes to sell provincially licensed tickets from an established point of sale or door-to-door in more than one municipality, it must send a letter of notification and copies of the licence and the licence application to each municipality in which it proposes to sell tickets.

entries.title: Provincial Licensing Procedures for Raffles
Amendments to Raffle Licences
entries.original_text:
A licensee must conduct a raffle lottery in accordance with its licence application and the terms and conditions of the licence. However, if it is in the public interest to do so, a licensing authority may issue an amendment to a licence it has issued, as long as no tickets have yet been sold (with the exception of Catch the Ace raffles as described in 5.2.1[l][v]). A licensing authority may also refuse a licence amendment request. A licensee must not change the way it is conducting and managing an event unless it has first obtained a licence amendment.

The licensing authority must consider requests for licence amendments on a case-by-case basis. The licensing authority is under no obligation to issue an amendment solely because it has previously issued an amendment under the same or similar conditions. Amendments must not be granted solely on the expectation of loss.

The licensee must apply for a licence amendment in writing before any tickets have been sold under the licence. The licensing authority will not grant amendments after ticket sales have begun. Expired licences may not be amended or cancelled.

If changes to the licence application package are made before submission to the licensing authority, each change must be initialled, on each document, by the individual(s) with signing authority for the applicant.

Requests for amendments to information already submitted to the licensing authority by way of completed application package must be made in writing on the applicant organization's letterhead and signed by the individual(s) authorized to bind the applicant. Any supporting records must be provided.

The licensing authority will only consider amending a raffle licence when it has discussed the reasons for the request with the licensee and established that the amendment is in the public interest.

entries.title: Requests to Cancel Raffle Licences
entries.original_text:
An organization licensed to conduct a raffle event may request to have its licence cancelled at any time, provided that no tickets have been sold. Any such request must be made in writing to the licensing authority and must state that no sales have taken place.

In the interests of consumer protection, a licence may not be cancelled once sales have begun except under the following conditions:
• all ticket purchasers must provide signed consent indicating that they have no objections to the lottery being cancelled; and
• the licensee must contact all ticket purchasers and refund the ticket purchase price.

The request to cancel must be made in writing to the applicable licensing authority with an explanation of why the licensee is making the request.

Unless all ticket purchasers agree to the above conditions, the licensee must conduct the lottery event as originally set out in the application for licence.

See also "3.4.1. Suspending or cancelling lottery licences".

entries.title: Financial Guarantees for Raffles
entries.original_text:
In order to safeguard the interests of both the public and the licensee, and to ensure that all prizes can be awarded, regardless of any extenuating circumstances, the licensing authority may request that an eligible organization present a financial guarantee covering the total value of the prizes. The licensing authority must require a financial guarantee for all fixed-prize raffle licences with a total prize value of $10,000 or greater, including taxes. The licensing authority may also require a financial guarantee when it determines it is appropriate, including for a prize amount less than $10,000 amount or where the prize is a percentage of the gross ticket sales.

When the total prize value of a fixed-prize raffle licence is $10,000 or greater, the licensing authority requires an irrevocable standby letter of credit as the form of financial guarantee.

For 50/50 draws, a letter of credit is not mandatory unless the licensing authority otherwise directs; however, where a letter of credit is not required by licensing authorities, another form of financial guarantee for prizes may be required when deemed appropriate.

See "3.6.7. Financial guarantees", and "3.6.8. Letters of credit" for further information.

entries.title: Raffle Advertising — General Guidelines and Ticket Sale Restrictions
entries.original_text:
The licensee is responsible for ensuring the accuracy of the information used in its advertising. The licensee should have its advertising materials reviewed by its legal counsel for compliance with all applicable federal and provincial legislation.

A municipality may only approve lotteries within its own boundaries and the Registrar may approve lotteries within Ontario. Therefore:
• raffle ticket orders must not be solicited or accepted from outside Ontario;
• raffle tickets must not be mailed or otherwise distributed to persons outside Ontario; and
• raffle tickets must not be advertised, promoted or marketed to persons outside of Ontario.

See "3.3.0. Advertising licensed lottery events" for further information.

entries.title: Raffle Advertising — Content Guidelines
Raffle Advertising — Celebrity Endorsements
entries.original_text:
Celebrities and other well-known personalities may endorse or otherwise promote raffles, but only if they provide such assistance to the licensee free of charge.

In addition, celebrity endorsement must not:
• state or imply that playing games of chance has contributed to the celebrity's success;
• be aimed at individuals under the age of 18; or
• specifically encourage individuals under the age of 18 to participate in a lottery event.

entries.title: Raffle Advertising — Print and Internet Requirements
Raffle Advertising — Mail Order Forms
entries.original_text:
Organizations conducting raffle lotteries often sell tickets through a mail order form built into an advertisement. The advertisement and the mail order form must both bear the lottery licence number. The consumer sees the advertisement, fills out the order form and mails it, along with the proper payment, to the charitable organization. The organization, in turn, records the purchase and mails the ticket to the consumer.

While the Registrar allows this procedure, organizations should be aware that it could cause administrative difficulties if the form is distributed in areas outside Ontario. Licensees must ensure that the mail order forms are not distributed beyond provincial boundaries nor should the licensee accept orders from outside Ontario.

entries.title: Raffle Advertising — National or International Media
entries.original_text: While a licensee may advertise in national or international newspapers and magazines, on the Internet or web sites, or on radio and television that may cross borders, tickets may only be sold in Ontario. A licensee may publish the winner's name and ticket number on the Internet.

entries.title: Gaming-Related Suppliers for Raffles
entries.original_text:
Call centres, ticket fulfilment centres and other organizations that provide a ticket order–taking service for licensed lottery events must be registered under the Gaming Control Act, 1992. Licensees that operate their own call centres must use their bona fide members and/or their own staff members, provided that the staff members' primary responsibility is not providing gaming services that would normally require registration.

If a licensee uses gaming services provided by an unregistered supplier or sub-contractor, the licensee will be in breach of the terms and conditions of the lottery licence.

entries.title: Duties of Bona Fide Members for Raffles
Raffle Prizes — Houses as Prizes
Raffle Prizes — Motor Vehicles
entries.original_text:
In addition to the general raffle lottery policies, the following policies apply to all motor vehicle raffle applications:
• The licensee must award the vehicle free of any hidden costs (such as taxes, pre-delivery inspection [PDI] costs and dealer's fees). This fact must be advertised. The licensee may choose to make the winner responsible for vehicle licence and insurance costs, provided these conditions are fully disclosed.
• The licensee may only award new vehicles, obtained through a registered dealer in Ontario, as prizes for raffle lotteries (except as detailed below). The dealer must be registered through the Ontario Motor Vehicle Industry Council.
• The licensee may not award leased or rental cars as prizes.
• The licensee must inform the winner of the point of delivery of the prize(s). The point of delivery must be in Ontario.
• The licence application must include a purchase agreement or a letter from the dealer indicating the type, model, and options of the car to be awarded and the full retail value of the car, including taxes, freight, PDI costs, duty and any other applicable costs.

entries.title: Raffle Prizes — Antique Motor Vehicles
Raffle Prizes — OLG Tickets as Prizes
entries.original_text:
Raffle lottery licensees may award Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation (OLG) tickets as secondary prizes for raffle lottery events provided that the following conditions are met:
• Any raffle advertising that includes OLG product names or logos must be directed to the OLG for approval. A letter of approval from the OLG must be submitted to the licensing authority.
• The OLG logo must be no more than half the size of the licensee's logo.
• The prize value of OLG tickets must be based on the face value (purchase price) of the tickets. The maximum value of these tickets must not exceed five (5) per cent of the prize board for the licensed lottery event.

entries.title: Raffle Prizes — Antiques and Art Objects
entries.original_text:
Occasionally, organizations conducting raffle lotteries offer antiques or art objects as the main prize for their event. In the interests of consumer protection and to ensure that any advertising represents the true market value of the item(s) offered, these types of prizes will be allowed only if the following documents accompany the application:
• a certificate from two independent, certified art/antique appraisers listing the appraised value of the object (value based on the lower appraisal);
• proof that the item is insured; and
• a statement of ownership.

entries.title: Raffle Prizes — Vacations
entries.original_text: Organizations conducting raffle lotteries may offer vacations as prizes, provided the travel agency is registered with the Travel Industry Council of Ontario (TICO). Where vacations are offered as prizes for raffle lotteries, the locations and dates of the arrivals and departures for all portions of the vacation package must be included in the licence application. The summary of the vacation package offered must include a full description of all services, including hotel accommodations, meals, complete travel arrangements including points of departure and entry (which must be in Ontario), limousine services, airline tickets, carriers and boat cruises.

entries.title: Raffle Prizes — Liquor
entries.original_text:
Liquor may be awarded as a prize for a raffle lottery provided that the organization conducting the raffle lottery ensures that:
• any such prize is purchased from the Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO), Brewers Retail (The Beer Store) or liquor manufacturer's retail store;
• any such prize is awarded only to persons 19 years of age or older; and that
• this condition is disclosed to all ticket purchasers.

entries.title: Raffle Prizes — Firearms
entries.original_text:
Prohibited and restricted firearms as defined in the Criminal Code (Canada) must not be used as prizes for raffle lotteries.

Licensees may only use non-restricted firearms (long guns) such as shotguns and rifles, which are reasonably used for hunting and sporting purposes, as prizes for raffle lotteries provided the following conditions are met:
• The licensee must ensure that the non-restricted firearm is retained and controlled by an individual who possesses the appropriate firearms licence for the purpose of transferring the non-restricted firearm.
• The non-restricted firearm must be properly registered. The transfer of the non-restricted firearm to the prizewinner must only occur after the prizewinner can produce a valid firearm Possession and Acquisition Licence, at which time the non-restricted firearm registration must be transferred to the prizewinner.
• The prizewinner has one year from the date of the draw in which to claim the prize and transfer registration of the firearm into the winner's name.
• In the event that the prizewinner does not obtain the required firearm licence, claim the prize and transfer registration of the firearm into the winner's name within one year, the firearm or monies equivalent to the fair market value of the firearm must be donated to a beneficiary approved by the licensing authority.
• The non-restricted firearm must be purchased or donated through an authorized firearms dealer in the province of Ontario.
• The licensee must have the conditions for receiving the prize printed on the ticket and fully disclosed to all raffle ticket purchasers.

entries.title: Raffle Prizes — Crossbows
entries.original_text:
Prohibited crossbows as defined in the Criminal Code (Canada) must not be used as prizes for raffle lotteries.

Licensees may only use crossbows that are reasonably used for hunting and sporting purposes as prizes for raffle lotteries. The crossbows must be purchased or donated through an authorized dealer in the province of Ontario.

entries.title: Unclaimed Raffle Prizes
entries.original_text:
Organizations that have conducted raffle lotteries and are unable to find the holder of the winning ticket(s) are obligated to make every reasonable effort to contact the winner, including:
• attempting to locate the winner through the telephone number and address on the ticket stub of the raffle ticket kept by the licensee, by sending a registered letter and telephoning; and
• advertising in local newspapers and/or radio stations within 120 days following the draw, setting out a complete list of the names and city of residence of all the winners of any unclaimed prizes.

The organization must keep records of the attempts made to contact the winner, as part of the documentation retained under the terms and conditions of the licence.

The unclaimed prize must be held in trust by the licensee for a period not less than six (6) months from the date the prize was awarded. At the end of the six-month period, the total amount of the prize held in trust, including interest, or the value of a merchandise prize equal to the manufacturer's suggested retail price, must be included by the licensee in the gross proceeds of the lottery event.
